style: Dry, medium to full body, oak/lees texture possible, ripe citrus and stone fruit. Medium lemon colour, butter and toast secondary notes.

conclusions: Drink now to medium-term ageing (5-7 years)

common_student_errors: Calling sweetness because of full body and oak
Listing oak but not explaining why it's there
Missing quality assessment based on texture and length
Confusing oak-derived aromas with primary fruit
Underestimating complexity due to oaky character

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: All Burgundy white is the same
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Chablis, Côte de Beaune, and Mâconnais each have distinct styles based on ripeness and winemaking
Well-integrated oak in Burgundy whites adds texture and complexity when balanced by acidity

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Full body and butter mean sweetness
misconceptions_addressed.correction: These are winemaking effects (MLF, oak, lees) not sugar; acidity confirms dryness

coaching_hints: Separate primary fruit from secondary winemaking notes.
Does the oak feel integrated or dominant?
How does acidity balance the richness of the wine?
What tells you this is quality despite the richness?

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Riper fruit (stone fruit instead of citrus only)
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Mâcon shows riper stone fruit than Chablis

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Butter, toast, creamy texture; increased complexity
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Secondary aromas of butter and toasted bread
